---
title: "GSoC 2026 Proposal: Time-Dependent Constraints in gfpop"
author: "William Zhang"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    latex_engine: xelatex
    keep_tex: false
geometry: margin=1in
fontsize: 11pt
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
  - \usepackage{amssymb}
  - \usepackage{hyperref}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "85%"
)
```

\newpage

<!-- ================================================================== -->
<!-- FRONT MATTER                                                       -->
<!-- ================================================================== -->

# Project Info

- **Project title:** Time-Dependent Constraints in gfpop
- **Project short title:** gfpop time-dep constraints
- **URL of project idea page:** <https://github.com/rstats-gsoc/gsoc2026/wiki/time-dependent-constraints-in-gfpop>

# Contact Information

- **Contributor name:** William Zhang
- **Postal address:** <!-- TODO: fill in -->
- **Telephone:** <!-- TODO: fill in -->
- **Email:** <!-- TODO: fill in (include Google-registered email) -->
- **GitHub:** [\@williamzhang7792](https://github.com/williamzhang7792)
- **Other channels:** <!-- TODO: Discord, Slack, etc. -->

# Contributor Affiliation

- **Institution:** <!-- TODO -->
- **Program:** <!-- TODO -->
- **Stage of completion:** <!-- TODO -->
- **Contact to verify:** <!-- TODO -->

# Schedule Conflicts

<!-- TODO: Be honest. List any jobs, classes, exams, travel, or other
     commitments during May 25 – Aug 24. Explain how you will manage
     the workload around them. -->

# Mentors

- **Evaluating mentor:** Vincent Runge <vincent.runge@univ-evry.fr>
- **Co-mentors:** Toby Dylan Hocking <tdhock5@gmail.com>, G Romano

**Have you been in touch with the mentors?**

<!-- TODO: Describe when and how — wiki edits, emails, test submissions.
     Be specific with dates. -->

\newpage

<!-- ================================================================== -->
<!-- THE PROPOSAL                                                       -->
<!-- ================================================================== -->

# Overview

<!-- Elevator pitch: ~0.5 page.
     - What is changepoint detection and why it matters.
     - What gfpop does (graph-constrained FPOP).
     - The gap: time-independent constraints.
     - What this project builds and why I want to build it. -->

Changepoint detection is a fundamental problem in statistics and signal
processing: given a sequence of observations, identify the points where
the underlying data-generating process changes. It arises in genomics
(peak detection in ChIP-seq data), finance (regime shifts), and climate
science, among other domains.

The \texttt{gfpop} package (Runge et al., 2020) generalizes classical
changepoint models by allowing transitions between segment states to be
constrained by a user-defined directed graph. Combined with the FPOP
pruning strategy (Maidstone et al., 2016), this yields an
$O(N \log N)$ solver for a wide family of constrained changepoint
problems — isotonic regression, up-down peak detection, and more — all
expressed through a single, elegant graph abstraction.

However, gfpop's constraint graph is \emph{time-independent}: the same
set of edges applies at every data point. This means it cannot express
models like LOPART (Hocking et al., 2022), where the allowed transitions
depend on whether the current data point falls inside a labeled region.
The proposed extension adds a single indirection — a rule function
$r: \{1, \ldots, N\} \to \{1, \ldots, R\}$ — that selects which subset
of edges is active at each time step, unifying both models within gfpop.

<!-- TODO: One sentence of personal motivation. Why does this project
     interest you specifically? -->


# The Problem: Why gfpop Cannot Express Label Constraints

<!-- ~1.5 pages. Concrete scenario → math → code → diagram. -->

## A Concrete Scenario

Consider a genomics researcher analyzing ChIP-seq data. They have
expert-labeled regions indicating where peaks should (positive labels)
and should not (negative labels) appear. They want a changepoint model
that respects these labels. The LOPART package can do this, but only for
Gaussian loss with no up-down constraints. gfpop can handle arbitrary
losses and graph constraints, but not labels. Why not?

## The Current gfpop Objective

The gfpop framework minimizes:

$$
Q_n = \min_{\substack{p \in \mathcal{G}_n \\ \mu \mid p(\mu)}}
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left[ \gamma_{e_t}(y_t, \mu_t) + \beta_{e_t} \right]
$$

where $\mathcal{G}_n$ is the set of valid paths through a
\emph{collapsed} constraint graph $G$, $\gamma_{e_t}$ is the loss
function on edge $e_t$, $\beta_{e_t} \geq 0$ is the penalty, and the
indicator constraints $I_{e_t}(\mu_t, \mu_{t+1}) = 1$ enforce
relationships between consecutive segment means (e.g., non-decreasing
for "up" edges).

The key limitation: the same graph $G$ applies at every time step $t$.

## The LOPART Objective

LOPART extends optimal partitioning by restricting the set of candidate
last-changepoints $T_t$ at each time step based on label positions. The
update rule has four cases depending on whether data point $t$ is:
unlabeled, inside a positive label, at the end of a positive label, or
inside a negative label. Each case allows a different set of transitions.

This is precisely a time-dependent constraint — and gfpop cannot express
it.

## The Gap in Code

In \texttt{Omega::LP\_edges\_operators(t)}
(\href{https://github.com/vrunge/gfpop/blob/master/src/Omega.cpp}{\texttt{src/Omega.cpp}}),
gfpop iterates over \emph{all} edges at \emph{every} time step:

```cpp
for (unsigned int i = 0; i < q; i++) {
  LP_edges[i].LP_edges_constraint(
    LP_ts[t][m_graph.getEdge(i).getState1()],
    m_graph.getEdge(i), t);
}
```

This is the single point in the codebase that makes gfpop
time-independent. The entire project reduces to making this loop
conditional on the current time step.

## Architecture Overview

<!-- TODO: Insert a TikZ or mermaid diagram here showing the full
     gfpop pipeline:
     R API → graph() / graphReorder() → gfpopTransfer() (Rcpp)
           → Omega::gfpop() → three-step DP loop
     Highlight LP_edges_operators as the modification target. -->

The gfpop pipeline flows from R to C++ as follows:

1. The user constructs a graph via \texttt{graph()}, \texttt{Edge()},
   \texttt{Node()}, \texttt{StartEnd()}.
2. \texttt{graphReorder()} sorts edges by \texttt{(state2, penalty)}
   and converts state names to 0-based integers.
3. \texttt{gfpopTransfer()} (the Rcpp bridge) reads the graph into
   C++ \texttt{Edge}/\texttt{Graph} objects and sets up the cost
   function dispatch via global function pointers.
4. \texttt{Omega::gfpop()} runs the DP with three steps per data point:
   - \texttt{LP\_edges\_operators(t)}: apply constraint operators to
     each edge's source cost function.
   - \texttt{LP\_edges\_addPointAndPenalty()}: add the current data
     point's cost and edge penalty.
   - \texttt{LP\_t\_new\_multipleMinimization(t)}: take the pointwise
     minimum across all incoming edges for each destination state.

The edge sort invariant — edges sorted by \texttt{(state2, penalty)} —
is exploited by step 3, which walks edges sequentially. Any
time-dependent filtering must preserve this invariant.

```{r architecture-diagram, fig.cap="gfpop architecture overview. The highlighted box is the single modification target for time-dependent constraints.", eval=FALSE}
# TODO: Generate or include the architecture diagram.
# Options: TikZ (via tikzDevice), a pre-rendered PNG, or ggplot2-based.
# knitr::include_graphics("figures/architecture.png")
```


# The Extension: A Rule Function

<!-- ~0.75 page. The mathematical formulation. -->

The proposed extension is minimal in concept: instead of applying all
edges at every time step, apply only the subset that matches the current
data point's rule.

## Definition

Define a rule function $r: \{1, \ldots, n\} \to \{1, \ldots, R\}$ that
maps each data point to a rule ID. Partition the edge set into $R$
subsets $E_1, \ldots, E_R$, where each edge belongs to the subset
corresponding to its rule ID. Edges with rule = NA belong to
\emph{all} subsets (backward compatibility).

## The Modified DP

The update equation becomes:

$$
Q^{s'}_{t+1}(\theta) = \min_{s : (s,s') \in E_{r(t+1)}}
\left\{ O^{s,s'}_t(\theta) + \gamma(y_{t+1}, \theta) + \beta_{s,s'} \right\}
$$

The only change from standard gfpop: the minimization is over
$E_{r(t+1)}$ instead of $E$.

## The Infinity Requirement

When state $s'$ has no incoming edges under rule $r(t)$, the cost
function must be $Q^{s'}_t(\theta) = +\infty$ for all $\theta$. The
constraint operators (\texttt{operatorUp}, \texttt{operatorDw},
min-envelope) must propagate infinity gracefully — this is the hardest
sub-problem and is discussed in detail in Section \ref{obstacles}.

Algorithmic complexity remains $O(KN)$ per step, with a smaller constant
when fewer edges are active under a given rule.


# What the User Sees: The R API

<!-- ~1 page. The front-end flex. -->

## Edge() Gains a rule Parameter

```r
gfpop::Edge("normal", "normal", type = "null", rule = 1)
gfpop::Edge("normal", "normal", type = "std",  rule = 1, penalty = 5.5)
```

Default: \texttt{rule = NA}, meaning the edge is active in all rules
(backward compatible with all existing gfpop code).

## gfpop() Gains a rule Argument

```r
gfpop::gfpop(data.vec, mygraph, type = "mean", rule = rule_vec)
```

The \texttt{rule\_vec} is an integer vector of length $N$, constructed
from the label annotations:

```r
LOPART.graph <- gfpop::graph(
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "normal",   type = "null", rule = 1),
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "normal",   type = "std",  rule = 1, penalty = 5.5),
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "normal",   type = "null", rule = 2),
  gfpop::Edge("noChange", "noChange", type = "null", rule = 2),
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "noChange", type = "std",  rule = 2),
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "normal",   type = "std",  rule = 3),
  gfpop::Edge("noChange", "normal",   type = "null", rule = 3),
  gfpop::Edge("normal",   "normal",   type = "null", rule = 4))

rule_vec <- data.table::fcase(
  unlabeled,          1L,
  in.positive.label,  2L,
  end.positive.label, 3L,
  in.negative.label,  4L)

result <- gfpop::gfpop(data.vec, LOPART.graph, type = "mean", rule = rule_vec)
```

## Backward Compatibility

When \texttt{rule} is omitted, all edges are active at all times —
current behavior is preserved. The \texttt{rule} column in the graph
defaults to NA; NA-rule edges are always active. Existing user code is
unaffected.

## Validation

- \texttt{length(rule)} must equal \texttt{length(data)}.
- Rule IDs in \texttt{rule} must be a subset of rule IDs in the graph.
- \texttt{graphReorder()} must preserve the \texttt{rule} column and
  maintain the sort invariant within each rule group.

## plotModel() Already Visualizes This

The \texttt{plotModel()} function from my Easy test already visualizes a
rule-partitioned graph as a state-by-rule transition matrix:

```{r plotmodel-output, fig.cap="State-by-rule transition matrix for the LOPART graph (4 rules, 2 states). Produced by plotModel() from the Easy test."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/plotModel_output.png")
```


# What the Solver Does: C++ Implementation

<!-- ~2 pages. Walk through each modification point with before/after. -->

## Edge.h — Add ruleID

Add an \texttt{unsigned int ruleID} field and \texttt{getRuleID()}
getter to the \texttt{Edge} class. The constructor reads it from the R
data frame's \texttt{rule} column. Edges with \texttt{rule = NA} receive
a sentinel value (\texttt{UINT\_MAX}) meaning "always active."

## Graph.h — Precomputed Rule-to-Edge Lookup

Two design options:

- **Option A (simple):** At each time step, loop over all $q$ edges and
  check \texttt{edge.getRuleID() == rule\_vec[t]}. $O(q)$ per step.
- **Option B (precomputed):** At construction, build a
  \texttt{std::vector<std::vector<unsigned int>{}>} mapping each rule ID
  to its sorted edge indices. At each time step, iterate only over
  \texttt{rule\_to\_edges[rule\_vec[t]]}. $O(\text{active edges})$ per
  step.

I recommend Option B. For the LOPART model, $q = 8$ total edges but only
2--3 are active per rule, so Option B avoids 60--75\% of the work per
step. The precomputed lookup must preserve the \texttt{(state2, penalty)}
sort order within each rule group so that
\texttt{LP\_t\_new\_multipleMinimization} continues to work correctly.

## Omega.cpp — LP\_edges\_operators(t): The Key Change

```cpp
// BEFORE (current gfpop)
for (unsigned int i = 0; i < q; i++) {
  LP_edges[i].LP_edges_constraint(
    LP_ts[t][m_graph.getEdge(i).getState1()],
    m_graph.getEdge(i), t);
}

// AFTER (with time-dependent rules)
for (unsigned int i = 0; i < q; i++)
  LP_edges[i].setInfinite();
const auto& active = m_graph.activeEdges(rule_vec[t]);
for (unsigned int idx : active) {
  Edge const& edge = m_graph.getEdge(idx);
  LP_edges[idx].LP_edges_constraint(
    LP_ts[t][edge.getState1()], edge, t);
}
```

First set all edges to infinity, then activate only those matching the
current rule. The \texttt{setInfinite()} method creates a single piece
with cost $= +\infty$ over the full domain.

## Omega.cpp — LP\_t\_new\_multipleMinimization(t)

No structural change needed. The current code walks edges sorted by
\texttt{state2} and takes the pointwise minimum via
\texttt{LP\_ts\_Minimization}. Infinite-cost \texttt{LP\_edges} will
always lose in the min-envelope, so states with no active incoming edges
naturally retain their infinite cost.

An early-out check should be added: if all incoming edges for a state
are inactive, skip the min-envelope to avoid merging infinity with
infinity.

## ListPiece.cpp — Infinity Propagation in Operators

The \texttt{operatorUp()}, \texttt{operatorDw()}, and unconstrained-min
operators currently assume finite input. When the input \texttt{ListPiece}
is the infinity sentinel:

- \texttt{operatorUp(infinity)} $\to$ infinity
- \texttt{operatorDw(infinity)} $\to$ infinity
- unconstrained min of infinity $\to$ infinity

Add an \texttt{isInfinite()} predicate on \texttt{ListPiece} and
short-circuit at the top of each operator. This is the trickiest part
of the implementation (see Section \ref{obstacles}).

## main.cpp — gfpopTransfer() Bridge

Read the \texttt{rule} column from the graph DataFrame (as
\texttt{IntegerVector}) and the \texttt{rule} argument from the function
call. Pass both to the \texttt{Omega} constructor. If \texttt{rule} is
not provided (R's default \texttt{NULL}), construct a vector of zeros and
mark all edges as belonging to rule 0.


# Two Models to Validate

<!-- ~1.5 pages. Each model is a self-contained vignette. -->

## LOPART in gfpop (4 Rule IDs, 2 States) {#lopart-model}

States: \texttt{normal}, \texttt{noChange}.

<!-- TODO: Include a state-rule matrix diagram (plotModel output or TikZ).
     Columns = rule IDs, Rows = states, Arrows = transitions. -->

Each rule corresponds to a label context:

- **Rule 1 (unlabeled):** Standard changepoint model.
  \texttt{normal} $\to$ \texttt{normal} (null + std).
- **Rule 2 (in positive label):** Allow at most one change.
  \texttt{normal} $\to$ \texttt{normal} (null),
  \texttt{noChange} $\to$ \texttt{noChange} (null),
  \texttt{normal} $\to$ \texttt{noChange} (std).
- **Rule 3 (end of positive label):** Exit \texttt{noChange}.
  \texttt{normal} $\to$ \texttt{normal} (std),
  \texttt{noChange} $\to$ \texttt{normal} (null).
- **Rule 4 (in negative label):** No changes allowed.
  \texttt{normal} $\to$ \texttt{normal} (null) only.

**Validation:** Oracle test against \texttt{LOPART::LOPART()}. On shared
test data (Gaussian loss, same penalty), the gfpop output with rule
vector must exactly match LOPART's output — same segments, same means,
same cost.

## Up-Down with Labels (6 Rule IDs, 4 States) {#updown-model}

States: \texttt{Up}, \texttt{Dw}, \texttt{noChangeUp},
\texttt{noChangeDw}. This model does not exist in any current R package.

<!-- TODO: Enumerate the 6 rules and edges active under each.
     Include a state-rule matrix diagram (4 rows × 6 columns).
     Include a filmstrip diagram: 6 panels, one per rule ID, each
     showing the active subgraph. -->

**Validation:** No oracle exists. Instead:

- Construct synthetic labeled genomic data with known peaks.
- Run gfpop with the 6-rule graph.
- Verify structural invariants: exactly one changepoint in each positive
  label region, zero changepoints in each negative label region.
- Compare unlabeled-region behavior against the standard gfpop up-down
  model (should match when all rules are set to the unlabeled rule).


# Obstacles and Challenges {#obstacles}

<!-- ~0.5 page. Be honest. -->

**Infinity handling in operators.**
The \texttt{operatorUp} and \texttt{operatorDw} methods in
\texttt{ListPiece.cpp} are the most complex code in gfpop ($\sim$200
lines each, intricate pointer manipulation on singly-linked lists).
Ensuring they correctly propagate and detect the infinity sentinel
without introducing memory leaks or segfaults will require careful unit
testing. I plan to write isolated test cases for each operator with
infinite inputs before integrating into the full DP.

**Edge sort invariant.**
The precomputed rule-to-edge lookup must maintain
\texttt{(state2, penalty)} ordering. If \texttt{graphReorder()} sorts
edges globally and then the rule lookup groups them by rule, the ordering
may be disrupted. I plan to sort edges by
\texttt{(rule, state2, penalty)} in \texttt{graphReorder()} and have the
lookup return contiguous sub-ranges.

**Backtracking through rule transitions.**
The backtracking code in \texttt{Omega::backtracking()} traces the
\texttt{Track} metadata stored in each \texttt{Piece}. When different
rules activate different edges, the backtracking must correctly identify
which edge was used at each time step. I need to verify that the existing
\texttt{Track} metadata (label=time, state=parent state) is sufficient,
or whether the rule ID also needs to be stored.

**Contingency.**
If the up-down+labels model (6 rules) proves harder than expected, I
will ensure the LOPART model (4 rules) is rock-solid with full test
coverage and benchmarks, and document the 6-rule model as future work
with a clear design spec.


# Non-Goals

- **Not extending to all loss functions this summer.** Focus on Gaussian
  loss first (what LOPART uses). Other losses are natural follow-ups but
  out of scope.
- **Not optimizing R-to-C++ data transfer.** The rule vector is passed
  as a plain integer vector. RLE compression is premature —
  correctness first.
- **Not modifying the PDPA algorithm.** Only the FPOP (penalty-based)
  solver.
- **Not redesigning gfpop's cost function dispatch.** gfpop uses global
  function pointers rather than a class hierarchy. I will work within
  this pattern, not refactor it.


# Capstone Deliverable: Vignette and Visualization

A formal, user-facing deliverable: an R vignette
(\texttt{vignettes/time-dependent-constraints.Rmd}) that:

1. Introduces the problem: "You have labeled data. Here's how to use
   it with gfpop."
2. Walks through a genomic peak detection example with labels.
3. Shows the \texttt{plotModel()} visualization of the rule-partitioned
   graph.
4. Compares gfpop output with LOPART output on the same data.
5. Includes a **filmstrip plot**: a sequence of panels showing the active
   subgraph at representative time points (one per rule ID).

The filmstrip visualization will be a formal deliverable — the kind of
artifact that makes a package actually usable, not just correct.


# Verification and Success Criteria

## Core Goals (must deliver)

- \texttt{Edge(..., rule=)} parameter added to R API, backward compatible
- \texttt{gfpop(..., rule=)} parameter added, backward compatible
- C++ edge filtering by rule in \texttt{LP\_edges\_operators}
- Infinity handling in all constraint operators
- LOPART model (4 rules, 2 states) working in gfpop, oracle-validated
  against \texttt{LOPART::LOPART()}
- Regression tests: gfpop without \texttt{rule} produces identical
  results to current gfpop
- \texttt{R CMD check} passes with no new warnings

## Stretch Goals (if time permits)

- Up-down with labels (6 rules, 4 states) working in gfpop
- Performance benchmarks: \texttt{gfpop(rule=...)} vs standalone LOPART,
  $N = 10^3$ to $10^6$
- Capstone vignette with filmstrip visualization
- Extension to Poisson loss with labels


# Timeline

<!-- Table format, like Eric Li's accepted AnkiDroid proposal. -->

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{llp{7.5cm}r}
\toprule
\textbf{Phase} & \textbf{Week} & \textbf{Details} & \textbf{Hours} \\
\midrule
Pre-acceptance & Now -- Apr 30 & Communicate with mentors, study gfpop C++ source, prototype infinity handling & N/A \\
\midrule
Community Bonding & May 1--7 & Fork gfpop, set up dev environment, configure CI & N/A \\
 & May 8--14 & Write design doc with mentors' input, finalize API & N/A \\
 & May 15--24 & Implement \texttt{Edge(rule=)} and \texttt{graph(rule=)} on R side & N/A \\
\midrule
Part 1 & May 25--31 & Add \texttt{ruleID} to Edge, rule vector to Omega, precomputed lookup & 30 \\
 & Jun 1--7 & Edge filtering in \texttt{LP\_edges\_operators}, infinity sentinel & 30 \\
 & Jun 8--14 & Unit tests: infinity through operatorUp, operatorDw, min-envelope & 25 \\
 & Jun 15--21 & Build LOPART graph in R, first end-to-end test with rule vector & 30 \\
 & Jun 22--28 & Oracle tests against \texttt{LOPART::LOPART()}, debug & 30 \\
 & Jun 29--Jul 5 & Hardening: edge cases, backward compat. Midterm report & 25 \\
\midrule
 & \textbf{Jul 6--10} & \textbf{Midterm: LOPART model working, oracle tests passing} & \\
\midrule
Part 2 & Jul 6--12 & Design 6-rule up-down graph, enumerate edges per rule & 25 \\
 & Jul 13--19 & Implement up-down+labels, validate on synthetic data & 30 \\
 & Jul 20--26 & Test label constraint satisfaction, 4-state edge cases & 25 \\
 & Jul 27--Aug 2 & Performance benchmarks, begin capstone vignette & 25 \\
 & Aug 3--9 & Extend plotModel for 6-rule graph, filmstrip visualization & 20 \\
 & Aug 10--16 & Polish: man pages, comprehensive test suite (fuzz, large N) & 20 \\
 & Aug 17--24 & Final code review with mentors, address feedback, submit PR & 15 \\
\midrule
 & \textbf{Aug 17--31} & \textbf{Final evaluation} & \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total} & & & \textbf{$\sim$330} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

**Contingency:** The vignette and visualization weeks (Jul 27 -- Aug 9)
are buffer. If the core implementation takes longer, the vignette scope
shrinks but the algorithm ships. If ahead of schedule, extend to Poisson
loss with labels.

**If behind schedule:** The 6-rule up-down model becomes a stretch goal.
LOPART (4 rules) with full test coverage and benchmarks is the minimum
viable deliverable.


# Management of Coding Project

- **Commits:** Daily pushes to a feature branch. PRs for each milestone:
  (1) R API + graphReorder, (2) C++ infrastructure + infinity,
  (3) LOPART model, (4) up-down model, (5) vignette.
- **CI:** \texttt{R CMD check} on GitHub Actions for every PR. The full
  testthat suite must pass before merge.
- **Communication:** Weekly status update to mentors via email or GitHub
  issue thread. If no commits for 3+ days, proactively flag blockers.
- **Code review:** Request mentor review at each milestone before
  proceeding to the next.
- **Red flag protocol:** If commits slow to $<$2/week, or tests fail
  without quick fixes, escalate to mentors immediately.


# Qualification Tests

All three tests completed. Repository:
\url{https://github.com/williamzhang7792/gsoc2026-gfpop-william-zhang}.
Both solvers are also integrated into a PeakSegOptimal fork:
\url{https://github.com/williamzhang7792/PeakSegOptimal}.

## Easy — plotModel(graph) Visualization

Wrote an R function that visualizes a gfpop graph augmented with a
\texttt{rule} column as a state-by-rule transition matrix using ggplot2.
Rows represent states, columns represent rules, and arrows indicate
allowed transitions. Self-loops are stacked vertically; edges are
shortened to avoid label overlap.

This visualization already models the proposed API extension — the
\texttt{plotModel()} output in Section 6 was generated by this function.

```{r plotmodel-test, fig.cap="plotModel() output from the Easy test: LOPART graph with 4 rules and 2 states."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/plotModel_output.png")
```

## Medium — Unconstrained FPOP (Poisson Loss)

Adapted PeakSegOptimal's 2-state constrained solver to a 1-state
unconstrained solver for optimal partitioning with Poisson loss. The key
insight: replaced \texttt{set\_to\_min\_less\_of} /
\texttt{set\_to\_min\_more\_of} with
\texttt{set\_to\_unconstrained\_min\_of} — a single flat piece at the
global minimum cost.

- 9 testthat tests, oracle-validated against
  \texttt{Segmentor3IsBack::Segmentor(model=1)} across multiple
  penalties.
- Integrated into the PeakSegOptimal fork with C registration,
  \texttt{.C()} interface, and 28 tests.
- Prepared a slide deck (\texttt{algo.pdf}) walking through FPOP pruning
  mechanics.

```{r pruning-demo, fig.cap="FPOP pruning trace from the Medium test, showing the number of piecewise cost function intervals over time.", out.width="75%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/pruning_demo.png")
```

## Hard — Regularized Isotonic Regression (Normal Loss)

Implemented \texttt{NormalLossPiece} with closed-form quadratic roots
(no Newton iteration needed, unlike the Poisson case). Both
\texttt{NormalLossPiece} and \texttt{PoissonLossPieceLog} inherit from a
shared \texttt{LossPiece} base class — directly relevant to gfpop's
multi-loss architecture.

Reconstructed \texttt{set\_to\_min\_less\_of} from first principles in a
standalone setting. This operator is the core of the isotonic constraint
and is the same operator that will enforce up-constraints in the
time-dependent extension.

- 14+ testthat tests including a 200-trial fuzz test against
  \texttt{isoreg()} (PAVA).
- \texttt{penalty=0} matches \texttt{isoreg}; positive penalty matches
  \texttt{fpop::Fpop()} on non-decreasing data.

```{r min-less-of, fig.cap="Visualization of the set\\_to\\_min\\_less\\_of operator from the Hard test. The blue curve is the original piecewise quadratic cost; the red curve is the running minimum.", out.width="75%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/set_to_min_less_of.png")
```

## What These Tests Demonstrate

The ability to read and adapt complex C++ numerical code ($\sim$1200
lines written), implement piecewise cost function operators (the exact
skill needed for this project), design for loss-function generalization
(shared base class), and validate rigorously against independent oracles.
The Medium and Hard tests exercise every component the time-dependent
extension will touch: the DP loop, the constraint operators, the
min-envelope, and the backtracking.


# About Me

<!-- ~1 page. Personal background, motivation, skills, commitments. -->

## Bio

<!-- TODO: Brief background. Why changepoint detection interests you.
     What drew you to this project. C++/R/algorithm experience. -->

## Personal Connection

<!-- TODO: Any experience with genomic data, time-series, or
     computational statistics? If not, that's fine — the test work
     speaks for itself. -->

## What I Expect from GSoC

<!-- TODO: Be genuine. Learning to contribute to a CRAN package.
     Collaborating with researchers. Becoming a long-term contributor. -->

## Other Commitments

<!-- TODO: Like Eric was about Ford — be honest about any job, classes,
     or commitments. Show discipline, not denial. -->

## Programming Languages and Tools

<!-- TODO: R, C++ (Rcpp), ggplot2, testthat, LaTeX, Git/GitHub.
     Numerical methods, optimization, computational geometry if relevant. -->


# References

- Runge, V., Hocking, T.D., Romano, G., Afghah, F., Fearnhead, P., and
  Rigaill, G. (2020). "gfpop: an R Package for Univariate
  Graph-Constrained Change-point Detection."
  \url{https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.03646}
- Maidstone, R., Hocking, T.D., Rigaill, G., and Fearnhead, P. (2016).
  "On Optimal Multiple Changepoint Algorithms for Large Data."
  \emph{Statistics and Computing}, 27(2), 519--533.
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1007/s11222-016-9636-3}
- Hocking, T.D. and Srivastava, A. (2022). "LOPART: Labeled Optimal
  Partitioning." \emph{Computational Statistics}, 38, 1461--1480.
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1007/s00180-022-01238-z}
- PeakSegOptimal. \url{https://github.com/tdhock/PeakSegOptimal}
- gfpop. \url{https://github.com/vrunge/gfpop}
